questions.prompt: What stop condition reliably keeps a Claude agent from running away?

choices.text: Always cap iterations with `maxSteps` (typical default 10–20). Pair with logging and alerts on consecutive tool errors. Hitting the cap should raise an error and either escalate to a human or return a partial answer — never silently retry.

questions.explanation: Bugs, ambiguous goals, or adversarial inputs can put any agent into a loop — and you pay per step. maxSteps is a hard ceiling. Combine with per-tool timeouts and approval gates on destructive tools for full safety.

questions.prompt: Your agent has an `email_customer` tool. In testing, it called the tool freely. What's the production-ready pattern?

choices.text: Add an approval gate in your tool runner: any call to an irreversible tool (email, charge, delete) requires explicit human confirmation before executing. The agent can still propose the action; the user clicks 'approve' before it runs.
Trust the model not to misuse it.

questions.explanation: Approval gates on irreversible tools are a non-negotiable production pattern. Sending emails, charging cards, deleting data — any of these should require human OK. The agent loop calls the tool runner; the tool runner checks for an approval before executing.
